does: This paper introduces BackdoorLLM, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating backdoor attacks and defenses in large language models performing open-ended text generation. The benchmark provides a unified repository with standardized training and evaluation pipelines, encompasses diverse attack modalities including data poisoning, weight poisoning, hidden-state manipulation, and chain-of-thought hijacking, and presents over 200 experiments across 8 distinct attack strategies. The work systematically characterizes how triggers embedded in inputs can manipulate LLMs to produce adversary-specified outputs, addressing a significant gap in security research that has largely overlooked text generation vulnerabilities despite extensive study of backdoor risks in vision and classification tasks.
connects: This work extends the established literature on backdoor attacks from computer vision and classification models to the understudied domain of generative language models. It builds on foundational work in adversarial machine learning and trigger-based attacks by adapting these concepts to the unique characteristics of LLMs and their open-ended generation capabilities. The paper connects to recent concerns about LLM safety and alignment, drawing on emerging research examining vulnerabilities in large-scale generative models. By providing a systematic benchmark, it responds to the need for rigorous evaluation frameworks similar to those developed for image classification but tailored to the distinct properties of text generation and the architectural features of modern transformer-based language models.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into both more sophisticated backdoor attack strategies against LLMs and the development of robust defense mechanisms. By establishing standardized evaluation protocols and a comprehensive dataset of attacks, it supports systematic exploration of which defense approaches prove effective across diverse threat scenarios. The work points toward developing certified defenses for language models, understanding the relationship between model scale and backdoor vulnerability, and creating detection mechanisms for poisoned generations. It also enables the community to evaluate whether defenses from other domains transfer to LLMs, and supports the development of more resilient training procedures and architectural modifications that can mitigate backdoor risks in production language models.
